title: Russian tea cakes
subtitle: Walnut shortbread balls rolled in icing sugar
excerpt: A crumbly, buttery walnut shortbread rolled into balls and coated in icing sugar twice: once hot from the oven, once cold. The double coating gives the characteristic thick, powdery crust.
sourceNotes: Russian tea cakes appear under many names across European and American baking traditions: Mexican wedding cookies, snowball cookies, Danish wedding cakes, and Austrian Kipferl all share the same structure of ground nuts folded into a buttery shortbread and rolled in icing sugar. The version labelled 'Russian tea cakes' is the American name, popularised through mid-century church and community cookbooks. Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (various editions from 1896) and Joy of Cooking (Rombauer, 2006 edition) both document the recipe.

content.text: The dough is very short (high fat to flour ratio) and will be crumbly before it comes together. Press and squeeze until it forms a cohesive ball; if it remains too crumbly to hold a shape, add cold water a teaspoon at a time. The walnuts should be ground to a fine rubble, not flour; a few larger pieces add texture.

The first icing sugar coating goes on while the biscuits are still hot and fragile: handle them gently. The sugar melts slightly against the warm biscuit and forms a thin glaze. The second coating, once cold, adds the thick powdery layer that defines the look.

attrs.prepNote: finely ground in a food processor

content.text: Make the dough and bake, 18 to 20 minutes

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{icing-sugar}} together until smooth. Beat in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Add the {{plain-flour}}, ground {{walnuts}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} and mix until the dough comes together. Roll into walnut-sized balls (about 12 g each) and place on the trays. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until the biscuits are set and very faintly golden on the base. The tops will remain pale.

Put the coating {{icing-sugar}} in a shallow bowl. While the biscuits are still hot, gently roll each one in the icing sugar and place on a rack. Leave to cool completely (about 30 minutes). Once cold, roll each biscuit in the icing sugar a second time, pressing gently so a thick coating adheres. Store in an airtight tin.
